skill.name: weather
skill.description: Provides weather lookup guidance for supported cities, with simulated results and notes for optional real API integration.
skill.summary: Query city weather using documented supported cities and simulated weather behavior.
skill.author: AI Creator

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, and obfuscation findings were false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline filenames, Chinese text, and normal documentation. The only remaining concern is low risk: weather_api.md links to external weather API documentation and describes future network/API-key integration, but the scanned files contain no executable implementation or prompt injection text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick locations are Markdown examples, inline filenames, or numbered instructions. No executable shell, Ruby, or Python code was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain Markdown code fences, example user prompts, or documentation filenames. There is no code path that executes commands.

low_findings.title: External Weather API Documentation Links
low_findings.description: weather_api.md links to OpenWeatherMap and WeatherAPI documentation and explains optional future API integration. This is not exfiltration, but a future implementation would need API-key handling, rate limiting, and network error controls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links and network notes are visible in documentation only. They indicate a legitimate weather use case, not unauthorized data transfer.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Crypto and Entropy Signals from Text Content
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography finding at SKILL.md line 3 points to a Chinese description string, not a cryptographic algorithm. The entropy finding at cities.md line 1 points to a Markdown heading in Chinese, not binary or encrypted content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown text. No hash function, cipher, encoded payload, binary blob, or encrypted content is present.

content.value_statement: Users need quick city weather answers without building a full weather integration. This skill documents supported cities, simulated weather behavior, and steps for adding real API data.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to query current weather by city name.
Documents example prompts for Beijing and Shanghai weather queries.
Lists five primary supported cities for weather responses.
States that unsupported cities return simulated weather data.
Documents optional integration steps for real weather APIs.
limitations: The current documentation describes simulated weather data, not live forecasts.
Only five primary cities are listed as supported.
No executable weather API client is included in the scanned files.
Future real API use would require secure API-key management.

use_cases.title: Quick City Weather Checks
use_cases.description: Ask for the current weather in a supported city during daily planning.

use_cases.title: Prototype Weather Assistant Flows
use_cases.description: Use the documented prompts and outputs to design a simple weather assistant.

use_cases.title: Plan Real API Integration
use_cases.description: Review provider options and implementation notes before adding live weather data.

prompt_templates.title: Ask Current Weather
prompt_templates.prompt: Query the current weather for Beijing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one supported city

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Cities
prompt_templates.prompt: Query the current weather for Shanghai and Guangzhou, then summarize the main differences.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Future Date
prompt_templates.prompt: Query the weather for Shenzhen tomorrow and explain whether the result is simulated or live.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare API Upgrade Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this weather skill and outline the steps needed to replace simulated data with a real weather API.

output_examples.output: Beijing weather: temperature, condition, and humidity are returned in a concise summary.
Shanghai forecast: the response includes the requested date and notes whether simulated data is being used.

output_examples.input: How can I connect a real weather API?
output_examples.output: The skill points to API-key setup, API calls, error handling, caching, and documentation updates.

best_practices: Tell users when a response uses simulated weather data.
Store future API keys outside prompts and documentation files.
Add caching and error handling before enabling live API requests.
anti_patterns: Do not present simulated data as verified live weather.
Do not paste API keys into skill documentation.
Do not add network calls without rate-limit and failure handling.

faq.question: Does this skill provide live weather data?
faq.answer: No. The current documentation says it uses simulated data.

faq.question: Which cities are listed as supported?
faq.answer: The skill lists Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Shenzhen, and Hangzhou.

faq.question: What happens for other cities?
faq.answer: The documentation says other cities return simulated data.

faq.question: Does it require an API key now?
faq.answer: No API key is required for the documented simulated behavior.

faq.question: Can it be connected to a real weather API?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation suggests OpenWeatherMap and WeatherAPI as possible providers.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found low risk documentation links and false positives, with no executable command behavior.
